---
title: "Numerical Analysis"
subtitle: "Foundations and Exercises"
author:
  - name: "TANAKA, Kazuaki (田中 一成)"
    email: "k.tanaka@aoni.waseda.jp"
    affiliations:
      - name: "Global Center for Science and Engineering, Waseda University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../030/custom.scss]
    slide-number: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    chalkboard:
      storage: chalkboardData_5th
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-image: "../030/images/image1.png"
      data-background-size: "contain"
      data-background-opacity: "0.15"
---

## Before We Start {.smaller}

```{=html}
<div class="mission-tip" style="margin-top:0.6em; margin-bottom:0.8em;">
  <div class="mission-tip-icon">📥</div>
  <div class="mission-tip-content">
    <strong>Please pull the latest course materials repository.</strong>
  </div>
</div>

<div style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; gap:0.5em; margin-bottom:0.8em;">
  <div>
    <div style="font-size:0.78em; color:#888; margin-bottom:0.2em;">Already cloned</div>
    <pre style="margin:0; background:#1e1e2e; color:#cdd6f4; border-radius:8px; padding:0.5em 1em; font-size:0.78em; line-height:1.5;"><code>cd materials
git pull</code></pre>
  </div>
</div>

<div style="background:#f0f4ff; border-left:3px solid #4472C4; border-radius:6px; padding:0.6em 1em; font-size:0.82em;">
  <strong>Today's materials</strong>:
  <table style="margin-top:0.4em; border-collapse:collapse; width:100%;">
    <tr><td style="padding:0.15em 0.6em 0.15em 0;"><code>050/5th.qmd</code></td><td style="color:#555;">slides — logistics and assignments</td></tr>
    <tr><td style="padding:0.15em 0.6em 0.15em 0;"><code>050/linear-equations-handout.qmd</code></td><td style="color:#555;">📘 <strong>shared handout for Lectures 5 and 6</strong></td></tr>
    <tr><td style="padding:0.15em 0.6em 0.15em 0;"><code>050/linear-equations-handout.html</code></td><td style="color:#555;">rendered shared handout</td></tr>
  </table>
</div>
```

## Today's Main Material {.smaller}

```{=html}
<div style="background:#e8f5e9; border-left:4px solid #2e7d32; border-radius:8px; padding:0.8em 1em; margin-bottom:0.7em;">
  <div style="font-size:0.82em; color:#1b5e20; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.04em;">LECTURE 5</div>
  <div style="font-size:1.05em; margin-top:0.2em;"><strong>Linear Systems I: Introduction to Stationary Iterative Methods</strong></div>
  <div style="font-size:0.84em; color:#455a64; margin-top:0.25em;">
    Use the shared Lectures 5--6 handout. Today we focus on Sections 1--9.
  </div>
</div>

<div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr; gap:0.8em;">
  <div style="background:#fff8e1; border:1px solid #ffe082; border-radius:10px; padding:0.75em 0.9em;">
    <div style="font-size:0.8em; color:#e65100; font-weight:700;">Today's scope</div>
    <ul style="font-size:0.83em; line-height:1.55; margin:0.4em 0 0 1.2em;">
      <li>systems of linear equations and conditioning</li>
      <li>direct methods vs iterative methods</li>
      <li>Jacobi method and Gauss-Seidel method</li>
      <li>basic implementation and numerical observation of residuals</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Reminder — Previous Assignments {.smaller}

```{=html}
<div style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; gap:0.55em; font-size:0.86em;">
  <div style="background:#fff3e0; border-left:4px solid #ef6c00; border-radius:6px; padding:0.55em 0.85em;">
    Ex 3.0--3.3 and Ex 4.0--4.3 are due <strong>May 14 (Thu), 23:59 JST</strong>.
  </div>
  <div style="background:#f3e5f5; border-left:4px solid #7b1fa2; border-radius:6px; padding:0.55em 0.85em;">
    Ex 3.2 video pair work will be handled in class when announced.
  </div>
</div>
```

## Exercise 5.0 — Evolve the Shared Handout with AI {.smaller .exercise-slide}

<div style="padding-left: 2em; margin-top: 0.5em; margin-bottom: 0.4em;">
  <strong>Repository:</strong> same as Ex 5.1
  &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; <strong>Submit:</strong> <code>linear-equations-handout.qmd</code> (your edited copy)
  &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; <strong style="color: #d84315;">Deadline: May 21 (Thu), 23:59 JST</strong>
</div>

While studying the shared Lectures 5--6 handout, insert at least
**3 Q&A blocks** of your own using the protocol in
[`AI_TUTOR.md`](https://github.com/waseda-num-analysis-2026/materials/blob/main/AI_TUTOR.md).

For Ex 5.0, focus on Sections 1--9.

## Exercise 5.1 — Understanding Matrix Condition Numbers {.smaller .exercise-slide}

<div style="padding-left: 2em; margin-top: 0.5em; margin-bottom: 0.4em;">
  <strong>Repository:</strong> <a href="https://classroom.github.com/a/0oWVw7DP">Ex 5 — GitHub Classroom</a>
  &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; <strong>Edit:</strong> <code>ex5-1.qmd</code>
  &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; <strong style="color: #d84315;">Deadline: May 21 (Thu), 23:59 JST</strong>
</div>

Your tasks are:

- use a concrete $2\times 2$ matrix example to explain geometrically
  what a large matrix condition number means
  (hint: it may help to draw how a matrix transforms the unit circle or
  the unit square $[0,1]^2$),
- create well-conditioned and ill-conditioned $2\times 2$ matrices,
- create $10\times 10$ matrices with good and bad matrix condition
  numbers,
- run the same perturbation experiment for those matrices and compare
  how strongly the well-conditioned and ill-conditioned matrices amplify
  input errors.

## Exercise 5.2 — Direct Solvers: `solve` vs `inv` {.smaller .exercise-slide}

<div style="padding-left: 2em; margin-top: 0.5em; margin-bottom: 0.4em;">
  <strong>Repository:</strong> same as Ex 5.1
  &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; <strong>Edit:</strong> <code>ex5-2.qmd</code>
  &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; <strong style="color: #d84315;">Deadline: May 21 (Thu), 23:59 JST</strong>
</div>

Using the matrices from Ex 5.1, compare `numpy.linalg.solve(A, b)` and
`numpy.linalg.inv(A) @ b` in terms of:

- computation time,
- relative residual,
- relative error of the solution,
- matrix condition number,
- and what these functions do internally.

Optional extension: investigate `scipy.linalg.solve(A, b, assume_a="gen")`,
including how the `assume_a` keyword argument lets SciPy use extra matrix
structure such as symmetric positive definiteness.

## Exercise 5.3 — Stationary Iterative Methods {.smaller .exercise-slide}

<div style="padding-left: 2em; margin-top: 0.5em; margin-bottom: 0.4em;">
  <strong>Repository:</strong> same as Ex 5.1
  &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; <strong>Edit:</strong> <code>ex5-3.qmd</code>
  &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp; <strong style="color: #d84315;">Deadline: May 21 (Thu), 23:59 JST</strong>
</div>

Implement two stationary iterative methods for solving $Ax=b$.

At this point in the course, you are not expected to give a convergence
analysis. The goal is to understand how the Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel
updates work, run them on prepared examples, and observe when their
behavior is similar or different.

In this report, you will:

1. implement the Jacobi method,
2. implement the Gauss-Seidel method,
3. test both methods on the prepared systems,
4. compare the number of iterations and residual histories.

## Assignments and Preparation {.smaller}

```{=html}
<div class="assign-list">
  <div class="assign-card">
    <div class="assign-num">1</div>
    <div class="assign-body">
      <p>Accept the <strong>Ex 5 GitHub Classroom</strong> link when announced.</p>
      <ul>
        <li>One repository will contain Ex 5.0, 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3</li>
        <li>The handout is shared with Lecture 6: <code>050/linear-equations-handout.qmd</code></li>
        <li><strong>Deadline:</strong> <span class="assign-due">May 21 (Thu), 23:59 JST</span></li>
      </ul>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```
